title: Is Meta Platforms Stock Going to $700 by Year-End? The Math Says It's Possible.
content:
Meta Platforms (META 0.80%) doesn't need to convince investors that it has been a top performer. The numbers prove it. In the past decade, this social media stock has climbed 461% (as of Mar. 13). It is one of the most valuable companies on the face of the planet today, with a market cap of $1.6 trillion.

Don't think that the momentum is over. There is still a reason to be bullish. Are Meta Platforms shares going to $700 by the end of the year? The math says it's possible.

Between 2020 and 2025, Meta's operating income increased at a compound annual rate of 21%. I don't think it's unreasonable to assume that this stellar pace of growth will decelerate this year. It might prove to be a worrying development for investors who are caught off guard.

That's because the leadership team expects Meta's costs and expenses to rise 41% year over year to $165.5 billion (at the midpoint) in 2026. This represents a notable acceleration from the 24% increase in 2025.

Given that sell-side analysts believe Meta's revenue will rise by 25% this year, an impressive leap at this scale, it looks as if there will be margin compression. That makes sense because the business has been spending a lot of money to bring in top-notch talent to pursue its artificial intelligence (AI) strategy, which isn't cheap. There are also the significant depreciation and amortization charges it has to report from its massive capital expenditures.

Combine 25% revenue growth with a 41% jump in costs and expenses, and Meta's operating income is set to increase by just 3% in 2026, leading to an operating margin of 34%. That's down from 41% in 2025 and 48% in 2024. This trend is worth paying attention to.

Profit growth isn't going to move the needle by much, if these forecasts prove to be correct. But investors shouldn't ignore valuation. Favorable market sentiment can boost stock prices in short order.

Right now, Meta shares trade at an enterprise value-to-earnings before interest and taxes (EV/EBIT) ratio of 19.4. In the past 12 months, the EV/EBIT multiple has averaged 21.4. Assuming the valuation gets back to its trailing one-year mean before 2026 comes to a close, then there's an additional 10% of upside that needs to be factored into this exercise.

Combined with the 3% gain from higher operating income, Meta's stock price could rise in the ballpark of 14% to approach $700 by year-end. If the financials are better than anticipated this year and investor enthusiasm grows, then shares could exceed these expectations.

*Average returns of all recommendations since inception. Cost basis and return based on previous market day close.

title: Vertiv Stock Just Spiked Higher. Wall Street Pros Say It's Not Done
content:
Vertiv (VRT), the AI infrastructure cooling company, has surged 50% year-to-date and trades at 43 times forward P/E with a Street-high target of $305 per share offering another 16% upside, as liquid cooling becomes essential for managing heat in data centers powering AI applications.

Hyperscaler tech giants are aggressively reallocating resources away from non-AI operations to fund accelerating data center spending, creating a structural demand floor for cooling infrastructure providers like Vertiv.

A recent study identified one single habit that doubled Americans’ retirement savings and moved retirement from dream, to reality. Read more here.

Shares of cooling infrastructure play Vertiv (NYSE:VRT) are starting to get hot again, now up more than 50% year to date. Undoubtedly, the stock might look a bit bubbly today, especially after the latest parabolic move. The AI infrastructure firm, now worth $101 billion, might also seem expensive after the latest run, but, arguably, it's looked pricey and overheated almost every step of the way.

With the firm joining the S&P 500 and numerous Wall Street analysts continuing to stand by the stock, even amid overbought conditions, questions linger as to whether it still makes sense to step in as a buyer here. Indeed, it's the AI infrastructure stock that's still working, even as the rest of the AI trade looks to hit the pause button for a while. With the latest spike coming after a far better-than-expected quarterly earnings result alongside an upbeat outlook, perhaps there is no better place to be as the orders keep on coming.

While shares of Vertiv certainly have a lot baked in after that strong earnings showcase, they certainly don't look obscenely priced at 77.4 times trailing price-to-earnings (P/E), especially given the order momentum, which could beget even more strength. On a price-to-sales (P/S) basis, the stock looks quite modestly priced at just shy of 10.0 times sales. And, on a forward P/E basis, the name might actually be a bargain at close to 43 times forward P/E if you're in the belief that AI is anything more than a bubble.

Read: Data Shows One Habit Doubles American’s Savings And Boosts Retirement

Most Americans drastically underestimate how much they need to retire and overestimate how prepared they are. But data shows that people with one habit have more than double the savings of those who don’t.

Either way, the picks and shovels continue to be the place to be and, in that regard, cooling infrastructure is a must. With hyperscalers committing hundreds of billions, Vertiv isn't a company that has a giant question mark surrounding its ROI. It's poised to make real cash flows, perhaps far sooner than most other AI plays that haven't yet proven themselves. In any case, the big question for investors is what happens if the big spenders just keep raising the bar on data center spending?

If cash flows, ROI figures, and job cuts come in from the biggest of spenders, perhaps the sky-high spend acts as the new floor going into the next year and the year after that. Could it be that the great multi-year AI data center buildout requires even more capital to feed the needs of the kinds of AI applications to come?

It's very much possible. But who knows what the reaction of investors will be as the hyperscalers get sent to the penalty box. In any case, cutting non-AI items to finance more aggression on AI seems to be an industry trend that I think won't slow down anytime soon. In my view, Jack Dorsey is a visionary who seems to know where the industry is headed next.

After steep cuts and rapid AI improvement going on behind the scenes, I view Dorsey's 40% staff cuts as a potential canary in the coal mine. With reports of 20% cuts over at Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META), perhaps the cost savings from AI aren't all too far off to justify not only the current spend (for 2026), but higher spend in 2027.

Liquid cooling isn't just a nice-to-have anymore. With the Vera Rubin boom around the corner and all these AI data centers that require their own grid, there's a lot of heat that needs to be dissipated. And perhaps Vertiv's high-margin runway may still be underestimated by investors.

At this juncture, the Street-high target on Vertiv stock sits at $305 per share. That's another 16% gain or so from here. Much of the positives from the last quarter might be priced in already. However, over the long run, I still think there's more room for more quarters like the one that caused the most recent spike. While cooling infrastructure might not be the "moatiest" way to play the data center buildout, I do think it's a wildly profitable one that might not be peaking quite yet. Either way, risks remain, and investors should brace for dips on the road higher.

Most Americans drastically underestimate how much they need to retire and overestimate how prepared they are. But data shows that people with one habit have more than double the savings of those who don’t.

And no, it’s got nothing to do with increasing your income, savings, clipping coupons, or even cutting back on your lifestyle. It’s much more straightforward (and powerful) than any of that. Frankly, it’s shocking more people don’t adopt the habit given how easy it is.

title: Meta vs. Netflix: One of These Stocks Will Double — and One Will Disappoint
content:
Meta Platforms (META) and Netflix (NFLX): Meta’s revenue growth exceeds 20% with expanding margins from its chip development, while Netflix’s ad-tier subscribers grow over 50% year-over-year with advertising revenue projected to exceed $3 billion in 2025, but Netflix trades at a premium 30x forward P/E multiple.

Meta’s investments in AI and Reality Labs offer long-term efficiency gains despite near-term headwinds, while Netflix faces growth constraints from the law of large numbers and intensifying competition that could limit upside despite trading at valuations reserved for best-in-class stocks.

Sending You to Google News in 3

I’m of the view the stock market is becoming much more bifurcated. Stock pickers may outperform in such an environment, with investors seemingly paying closer attention to fundamentals than I’ve seen in some time.

With that in mind, I thought I’d compare and contrast two of the top Magnificent 7 stocks, to determine which may be the better pick for long-term investors. Let’s dive into whether Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META | META Price Prediction) or Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) will come out on top as the company that has double-up potential from here.

Meta Platforms’ latest results underscore what long‑term investors should really care about. This is a company with one of the most dominant online followings, capturing nearly half of the world’s eyeballs on a daily basis on one of its core social media platforms. With an incredible ads franchise driving impressive margins (which have continued to improve thanks to Meta’s focus on efficiency), Meta’s status as a cash cow can’t be ignored.

Now, that’s not to say this isn’t a company without its fair share of headwinds. Outside of the macro climate, the company’s launch of four AI chips didn’t resonate with investors this past week. Additionally, other reports that Meta is delaying its next-generation large-language model (Avocado) sent shares lower, given how much the social media giant has spent (expected to spend well more than $100 billion this year on these ambitions). And to cap off concerns, billionaire investor Stanley Druckenmiller announced he’s exited his position in Meta, furthering concerns among investors on Main Street.

My view on Meta remains bullish, despite these concerns. I think Meta’s revenue growth rate (which has been north of 20%) should continue, and margins should expand as a result of the company’s push to develop its own chips. Over the long-term, I think these are solid investments, and those who have the willingness and ability to be patient with this period of time could be well rewarded.

Yes, Reality Labs and AI spending levels are very high, and will likely provide near-term headwinds. That said, over the long-term, I think these will ultimately turn out to be solid investments in productivity and efficiency, which investors will clearly benefit from.

Netflix is the streaming giant that really requires no introduction. The company has successfully reinvented its growth algorithm, shifting from a pure subscription story to a more diversified model anchored by an ad‑supported tier, paid sharing, and selective pushes into live events, sports, and gaming. The ad tier is the headline driver right now. Indeed, ad‑supported viewing already makes up a substantial share of total hours, with ad‑tier subscribers growing north of 50% year‑over‑year and advertising revenue projected to exceed $3 billion in 2025. That is high‑margin, scalable revenue layered on top of a still‑expanding global subscriber base.

Now, that’s not to say there aren’t risks with owning Netflix stock here. The company is trading at a forward price-earnings ratio of more than 30-times, and also 9-times sales. Those multiples are reserved for only the best of the best stocks, which I’d argue Netflix should be a part of.

That said, with the company recently pulling back from its WarnerBros deal, and recently inking a deal to acquire Ben Affleck’s AI film company InterPositive, the expectation is that further job cuts could be on the horizon. Such moves could enhance productivity and efficiency, and lead to further gains – that’s the bull case here.

I think Netflix will continue to be able to leverage AI more than most of its Magnificent 7 peers, but the question is about what kind of future return investors will see from these investments. Right now, the jury is out on this stock.

Accordingly, it’s my view that between the two, Netflix is more likely to “disappoint” relative to lofty growth expectations simply because the law of large numbers and intensifying competition cap its upside, even as it remains a fundamentally sound hold‑forever media name.

Chris MacDonald is a 24/7 Wall St. contributor and long-time contributor to other notable finance publications, including The Motley Fool and InvestorPlace. With an MBA in Finance, and more than a decade of experience in venture capital and the corporate finance world, Chris brings a long-term perspective to his analysis of equities and alternative assets.

His love of investing and focus on finding quality undervalued stocks is complemented by recent research into alternative assets as well. He takes a long-term approach to analyzing companies and cryptos, with a focus on directing the reader to the most sustainable and important catalysts for each respective potential investment.

title: Nasdaq Today: Tech Composite Leads US Indices Higher as Nvidia GTC Ignites AI Optimism and Micron Surges Ahead of Earnings
content:
The Nasdaq Composite outperformed both the S&P 500 and Dow Jones on Monday, March 16, 2026, rising approximately 1.2% to trade around 22,370-22,430 by midday — its strongest single-session performance since the Middle East conflict began rattling global equity markets.

After closing Friday at 22,105.36, down 0.93% in a session that marked one of the tech index's weakest weekly closes of the year, Monday's recovery reflected a powerful convergence of catalysts that directly targeted the growth and technology companies that dominate the Nasdaq's composition.

Yahoo Finance's live data confirmed the Nasdaq Composite at 22,386.78, up 1.27%, with Nasdaq 100 futures having been 1% higher in pre-market trading as investors responded to three simultaneous positive signals: the partial Strait of Hormuz reopening for LPG tankers, the commencement of Nvidia's GTC AI Conference in San Jose, and reports of Meta's planned AI-driven workforce reduction. Together, these catalysts demonstrated that the structural AI growth narrative driving Nasdaq valuations had not been disrupted by the Middle East conflict — it had merely been temporarily suppressed.

The headline event for Nasdaq investors on Monday was the commencement of Nvidia's annual GTC AI Conference in San Jose, California — widely regarded as the most important technology conference of the year for AI hardware and infrastructure investors. CEO Jensen Huang's keynote, expected to focus on next-generation GPU architectures, AI agent software platforms, and quantum computing partnerships, set the stage for days of product announcements and partnership reveals that will have direct implications for Nvidia's revenue trajectory in 2026 and 2027.

Nvidia rose more than 2% on Monday, with pre-market gains of 1.5% accelerating through the session as conference-driven optimism spread. Advanced Micro Devices — Nvidia's primary competitor in AI training chips — also advanced 2.1%, benefiting from the AI sector's rising tide even as it competes directly with Nvidia for data centre workloads. AMD's continued challenge to Nvidia's dominance in AI training, combined with its growing success in AI inference applications, has established it as the second-most important semiconductor bellwether for the Nasdaq's technology sector.

Micron Technology's 5-6% advance on Monday was arguably the session's most important single stock development for the Nasdaq's near-term trajectory. The memory chip maker is scheduled to report earnings after Wednesday's market close, and the size of Monday's pre-earnings advance reflects the degree of institutional confidence in a strong result. The combination of TSMC's 30% revenue growth for January-February, confirmed the prior week, the AI data centre's insatiable demand for high-bandwidth memory chips, and the semiconductor industry's broader pricing recovery from the 2023-2024 inventory correction cycle all point toward a potentially exceptional Micron earnings report.

Micron's results on Wednesday will be the Nasdaq's single most important earnings event of the week. A beat with strong forward guidance would likely push Micron's stock further and generate broad semiconductor sector momentum, lifting Intel, Qualcomm, and other Nasdaq-listed chip companies alongside it. It would also validate the thesis articulated by Wedbush's Dan Ives and other technology bulls that the AI semiconductor cycle is fundamentally intact despite the temporary disruption of geopolitical risk.

Among the Nasdaq's most remarkable individual movers on Monday was Intel Corporation, which surged 6.29% to lead the S&P 500's entire 500-stock constituent universe. Intel's dramatic single-day advance reflects a combination of factors: relief buying after a period of deep underperformance, optimism about the company's AI chip product roadmap following GTC conference competitor announcements that often clarify the competitive landscape, and growing investor confidence that Intel's manufacturing partnership with TSMC — which provides foundry services for Intel's most advanced chip designs — positions it to participate more fully in the AI hardware cycle than previous generations.

Intel had been one of the Nasdaq's most challenged major components through 2025 and into early 2026, as the company underwent a significant strategic transformation under CEO Pat Gelsinger. The turnaround involves rebuilding Intel's manufacturing competitiveness, launching new AI-capable chip architectures, and establishing itself as a viable alternative to TSMC for US and European semiconductor customers seeking supply chain diversification. Monday's 6% surge suggests that at least some institutional investors believe that transformation is beginning to gain tangible traction.

Meta Platforms' nearly 3% advance on Monday — driven by reports that the company was considering laying off up to 20% of its workforce due to AI efficiency gains — encapsulates one of the most consequential narrative shifts in the technology sector in 2026. In previous eras, a 20% workforce reduction announcement would be interpreted as a sign of company distress, triggering stock price declines and questions about management confidence in future growth. In the current AI productivity era, the market interprets the same announcement as evidence of accelerating AI monetisation and margin expansion.

The logic runs as follows: if Meta can maintain or grow its revenue output while employing 20% fewer people, its profitability will increase substantially. AI tools handling content moderation, advertising optimisation, product development, and customer service functions that previously required human labour represent genuine, permanent cost savings rather than cyclical expense reductions. For the Nasdaq Composite — which has Meta as one of its most heavily weighted components — this narrative shift is material for the index's earnings growth outlook over the next twelve to twenty-four months.

Block, the fintech company led by Jack Dorsey, saw its stock price surge on Monday after the company announced what analysts described as a 'radical bet' on decentralised infrastructure — a move that positioned Block as a technology-forward financial infrastructure company rather than a traditional payments processor. While Block's size makes it a secondary rather than primary Nasdaq driver, its dramatic advance contributed to the breadth of Monday's technology sector recovery and reinforced the narrative that innovative technology companies are finding ways to create value despite the geopolitical headwinds dominating the macro backdrop.

The week also saw a notable corporate development from Micron Technology, which announced plans to build a second US manufacturing facility — a strategic decision directly enabled by the CHIPS and Science Act funding and the growing urgency among US policymakers to build domestic semiconductor manufacturing capacity. The announcement reinforced both the AI investment supercycle thesis and the domestic semiconductor resilience narrative that has been one of the Nasdaq's structural supports throughout the current period of global uncertainty.

Of all the major US equity indices, the Nasdaq Composite has the highest sensitivity to Federal Reserve interest rate decisions and forward guidance. Technology and growth stocks are long-duration assets whose present value is particularly sensitive to discount rate changes, meaning that even small shifts in the Fed's implied rate path can generate outsized Nasdaq moves relative to the Dow or S&P 500. Wednesday's FOMC decision and Powell's press conference will therefore be the week's most market-sensitive event for Nasdaq investors.

March 16, 2026 is a day on which the Nasdaq Composite has reminded markets why it delivered more than 20% gains in 2025 and why institutional investors continue to view technology as the dominant structural investment theme of the decade. The 1.2-1.27% advance, led by Nvidia's GTC excitement, Micron's pre-earnings surge, Intel's dramatic single-day reversal, and Meta's AI efficiency narrative, demonstrates that the AI growth story has not been permanently interrupted by the Middle East conflict — it has been temporarily tested. The week's remaining catalysts — the Fed decision, Micron's earnings, and FedEx's logistics read — will determine whether Monday's recovery marks the beginning of a sustained Nasdaq recovery or another short-lived relief rally in a volatile geopolitical environment. The VIX's decline to 25.17 from the prior week's 26-27 range suggests the former is becoming more probable.

Felix is the founder of Society of Speed, an automotive journal covering the unique lifestyle of supercar owners. Alongside automotive journalism, Felix recently graduated from university with a finance degree and enjoys helping students and other young founders grow their projects.
